query: Are there any colorful beetles in Spain?

response.answer:
To determine if there are any colorful beetles in Spain, we would need to look at the beetle species that inhabit the region. While I don't have real-time access to specific databases or recent studies, I can provide some general information based on known beetle diversity.

Spain hosts a diverse array of beetle species, and many of them can be quite colorful. For example:

1. **Chrysomelidae (Leaf Beetles)**: These are often brightly colored and include species like the **Cassida ruficornis** (commonly called the "red-headed leaf beetle"), which is known for its metallic green or blue coloration.

2. **Scarabaeidae (Scarab Beetles)**: This family includes several colorful species, such as the **Lucanus cervus** (European stag beetle), though it's more notable for its size and less so for its color.

3. **Buprestidae (Metallic Wood-Boring Beetles)**: These beetles are known for their metallic sheen in various colors like green, blue, or bronze. Species such as the **Chrysochroa fulgidissima** can be found in Spain and exhibit striking coloration.

4. **Melyridae (Robin Beetles)**: Some species within this family have vibrant colors, though they are generally smaller than other beetles.

5. **Elateridae (Click Beetles)**: The genus **Hemicrepidius**, which is mentioned in the provided documents, includes colorful species that can be found in Spain.

For a more detailed and up-to-date list of colorful beetle species in Spain, you might want to consult local entomological resources or contact a specialist in Spanish beetle fauna. Museums, universities with biology departments, and online forums dedicated to insect enthusiasts could also provide valuable information.

treatments.articleTitle: A taxonomic review of the genus Isocopris Pereira and Martínez, 1960 (Coleoptera: Scarabaeidae: Scarabaeinae), with description of a new Brazilian species
treatments.speciesDesc: Isocopris inhiatus is a species of beetle in the family Cerambycidae. It is described as large, with a body length of 25-36 mm and a shiny black to dark brown coloration. The male has a distinctive yellow to orange antennal club, while females have a head margin that is slightly notched at the clypeo-genal junction.

treatments.articleTitle: The variegated mud-loving beetles (Coleoptera: Heteroceridae) of Mississippi and Alabama, with discussion and keys to the species occurring in the southeastern United States
treatments.articleAuthor: King, Jonas G. & Lago, Paul K.
treatments.speciesDesc: Heterocerus pallidus Say is a species of beetle in the family Cerambycidae. It is commonly found in eastern North America, with a range that extends from New England to southern Alberta and south to eastern Arizona and the coast of Mississippi. The species is characterized by its dark brown to yellowish-brown coloration, pale elytra markings, and hypermandibulate male genitalia.

treatments.articleTitle: Lifting the blue-headed veil - integrative taxonomy of the Acanthocercus atricollis species complex (Squamata: Agamidae)
treatments.articleAuthor: Wagner, Philipp, Greenbaum, Eli, Bauer, Aaron M., Kusamba, Chifundera & Leaché, Adam D.
treatments.speciesDesc: Acanthocercus minutus is a small species of Agama found in Ethiopia and Kenya. It has a distinctive brownish color with a reticulated blue pattern on the throat, similar to other members of its genus. The species is primarily restricted to dry savannah and woodland habitats at altitudes below 3000m asl.

images.captionText: Figure 17. Holotype (ZMB 29089) of Acanthocercus minutus, from Dscheffedenza, Ethiopia.

treatments.articleTitle: Illustrated identification guide to the Nordic aphids feeding on Conifers (Pinophyta) (Insecta, Hemiptera, Sternorhyncha, Aphidomorpha)
treatments.speciesDesc: Cinara pini is a species of fig (Ficus pini) that is known for its distinctive wax markings and colonies on young shoots, twigs, and branches in open forests and meadow margins. It is a holocyclic, monoecious species that forms small colonies with ants, including Camponotus herculeanus and Formica aquilonia. The figs are typically greyish-brown to brown or olive-brown in color with bronze sheen and thin wax markings along the mid-line and segment borders.

images.captionText: Fig. 89. Cinara (Cinara) pini (Linnaeus, 1758). Freeze-dried apterae. A–B. Adults. C. Full-grown juvenile.

treatments.articleTitle: Brachymelecta Linsley, 1939, previously the rarest North American bee genus, was described from an aberrant specimen and is the senior synonym for Xeromelecta Linsley, 1939
treatments.articleAuthor: Onuferko, Thomas M., Packer, Laurence & Genaro, Julio A.
treatments.speciesDesc:
The answer to the question "What is the synonym for Brachymelecta californica?" is:

B. mucida (also known as Melecta miranda)

This is because the original description of B. californica was based on a specimen that was later found to be identical to M. miranda, and subsequent descriptions have used this name as a synonym for B. californica.

images.captionText: Fig. 1. Specimen labels. A. Label associated with a male specimen (Fig. 8A) in which both fore wings have two submarginal cells, found among the collection of ‘Xeromelecta californica’ specimens at the ANSP. B. Labels associated with the Melecta? mucida holotype, ♂ (ANSP 2294) (herein synonymized under Brachymelecta californica (Cresson, 1878)).

Fig. 5. Brachymelecta californica (Cresson, 1878). A. Female (PCYU PYU-2276), habitus, lateral view. B. Female (BOLD sample ID: CCDB-34570 A07; PCYU), habitus, dorsal view. C. Male (BOLD sample ID: CCDB-34570 A11; PCYU), habitus, lateral view. D. Female (PCYU PYU-2391), axillae and mesoscutellum, dorsal view (blue arrow indicates one of two short, mammiform tubercles of mesoscutellum).

Fig. 6A–C (continued on next page). Dissected male S7 (left) and S8 (right), ventral view (with anterior ends oriented toward the top). A. Brachymelecta californica (Cresson, 1878) (photo of Melecta californica paralectotype (ANSP 2292-5)).B. B. californica (photo ofM.? mucida holotype (ANSP 2294) (herein synonymized under B. californica)) (S8 appears to have been damaged/split anteromedially). C. B. haitensis (Michener, 1948) (JAG).

Fig. 7. Melecta? mucida holotype, ♂ (ANSP 2294) (herein synonymized under B. californica (Cresson, 1878)). A. Habitus, dorsal view (blue arrows indicate apical fasciae of metasomal terga). B. Metasoma, dorsal view (blue arrows indicate apical fasciae of metasomal terga) (pubescence appears to be matted from prior wetting). C. Habitus, lateral view. D. Head, frontal view.

Fig. 8. Brachymelecta californica (Cresson, 1878), habitus, dorsal view. A. Male specimen (ANSP) from Nevada (bearing the same ‘Nev’ collection label as the Melecta? mucida holotype) in which both fore wings have two submarginal cells. B. M. californica paralectotype, ♂ (ANSP 2292-7) in which the left fore wing has three submarginal cells and the right has two, with the second submarginal cross vein incomplete. C. M. californica paralectotype, ♂ (ANSP 2292-4) in which the left fore wing has two submarginal cells and the right has three (note that in both fore wings, the second submarginal cross vein (incomplete on the left, complete on the right) originates from the first submarginal cross vein, not Rs as is typical for most bees, including this species). D. Female specimen (BOLD sample ID: CCDB-34570 A03; PCYU) in which the discs of the metasomal terga are (unusually for B. californica) covered in short, pale hairs, sparser than those comprising the apical fasciae.

treatments.articleAuthor: Don E. Wilson & Russell A. Mittermeier
treatments.journalTitle: Handbook of the Mammals of the World – Volume 1 Carnivores
treatments.speciesDesc:
The text appears to be a scientific article or study about the Aardwolf, a nocturnal mammal native to southern Africa. Here's a summary of the main points:

**Physical Characteristics and Behavior**

* The Aardwolf is a medium-sized mammal with a stocky body, short legs, and a long, pointed snout.
* It has a distinctive mane of hair around its neck, which it uses for defense and communication.
* Aardwolves are nocturnal, meaning they are active at night, and have poor eyesight but excellent hearing.

**Habitat and Distribution**

* The Aardwolf is found in southern Africa, from South Africa to Mozambique.
* It inhabits a variety of habitats, including grasslands, savannas, and woodlands.

**Reproduction and Social Structure**

* Aardwolves are monogamous, with pairs forming during the mating season (June-July).
* Females give birth to 1-4 cubs per year, which are born in a den.
* The cubs stay with their mother for about three months before weaning.

**Conservation Status**

* Listed as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List due to its shy and nocturnal nature, making it difficult to detect and study.
* However, Aardwolves are threatened by indirect poisoning from locust outbreaks, predation by Black-backed Jackals, human persecution for food or sport, and habitat fragmentation.

**Interesting Facts**

* Aardwolves have a unique vocal repertoire, with nine distinct sound types, including "purr", whine, jaw click, lip smack, growl, snarl, bark, squeal, and a whizzing sound.
* They are also known for their ability to dig burrows quickly and efficiently.

Overall, the Aardwolf is an interesting and unique animal that requires careful conservation efforts to protect its habitat and reduce threats from human activities.

treatments.articleTitle: Revision of the Metallactus kollari species-group with a new diagnosis of the genus (Coleoptera: Chrysomelidae: Cryptocephalinae)
treatments.speciesDesc: Metallactus rileyi is a species of fig that belongs to the subfamily Mymaridae, which is part of the family Chrysomelidae. It is characterized by its medium size, tronco-conical pronotal outline, and yellow or slightly darkened abdominal sternites with a black pattern featuring two oblique spots separated by a yellow median band. The species has distinct differences in its male and female specimens, including differences in the shape of the eyes, antennae, and the structure of the aedeagus.

treatments.articleTitle: Revision of the Australian species of Figulus MacLeay, 1819 (Coleoptera: Lucanidae)
treatments.articleAuthor: Monte, Cinzia, Zilioli, Michele & Bartolozzi, Luca
treatments.speciesDesc:
The question asks for the distribution and natural history data of the insect Figulus lilliputanus Westwood and Figulus clivinoides Thomson, Bomans. The correct answer is:

Distribution: Australia (Australian Capital Territory, New South Wales, Queensland, Western Australia, Victoria, South Australia)

Natural history data:
* Adults were collected throughout the year, mostly from December to April.
* Most of them were collected at light trap with mercury vapor light; a single specimen was captured in a flight intercept trap (Hann River, XII.1993 – I.1994).
* Few other specimens were found in rotten logs, in vine scrub.
* The elevation ranges from 50 to 390 m.
* The larvae and host plant(s) are unknown.

Note that the question also mentions Tasmania as a possible locality for F. clivinoides, but this is not confirmed.

images.captionText: FIGURES 8‒11. Figulus lilliputanus Westwood. 8, dorsal view of adult (scale bar: 5 mm); 9, mentum; 10, male genitalia, dorsal view (scale bar: 0.5 mm); 11, male genitalia, lateral view.

treatments.treatmentTitle: Rosalba tanimbuca Galileo & Martins, 2013, sp. nov.
treatments.articleTitle: Nine new species of the genus Rosalba (Coleptera, Cerambycidae, Lamiinae, Apomecynini) from South America
treatments.articleAuthor: Galileo, Maria Helena M. & Martins, Ubirajara R.
treatments.speciesDesc: Rosalba tanimbuca is a new species of beetle described by Melzer in 1934. It has distinctive features such as reddish-brown integument with whitish pubescence, deep and close punctures on the vertex, and yellowish pubescence on the posterior third of the elytra. The species differs from other Rosalba species, including R. bucki and R. malleri, by its unique characteristics.

treatments.articleTitle: An annotated checklist of Dolichopodidae (Diptera) species from Iran, with new records and a bibliography
Catalogue of Tortricidae Latreille, 1802 (Lepidoptera: Tortricoidea) of India
treatments.articleAuthor: Pathania, Prakash C., Das, Apurva & Chandra, Kailash
treatments.speciesDesc: Matsumuraeses melanaula is a species of moth in the family Eucosmatidae, commonly known as exotic microlepidoptera. It was first described by Meyrick in 1916 and is found in India, Nepal, and other parts of Asia. The larvae feed on flowers and seed pods of various legume species, including Cajanus indicus and Phaseolus mungo.

treatments.articleTitle: Trichrysis baratzsensis sp. nov. (Hymenoptera: Chrysididae) from Sardinia *
treatments.speciesDesc: Trichrysis baratzsensis is a newly described species of wasp in the genus Trichrysis. It is characterized by distinctive features such as the absence of pronotal lateral carina, punctures on mesonotum and scutellum, and tridentate distal margin of TIII. The species was found exclusively in Lake Baratz in Sardinia, Italy, and its habitat is described as a sandy shore with thick vegetation surrounding a permanent lake basin.

It differs from other members of its genus, Gongylosoma, and other genera such as Cyclophis, Longicaudum, Mukutense, Nicobariense, Scriptum, and Theobaldia, due to several distinct morphological characteristics.

treatments.treatmentTitle: Heterostemma bicanthaceum Meve, Gateble & Liede. A 2018, sp. nov.
treatments.articleTitle: Two new species from the Ile des Pins (New Caledonia), and a not so new species from Grande Terre (New Caledonia)
treatments.articleAuthor: Meve, Ulrich, Gâteblé, Gildas & Liede-Schumann, Sigrid
treatments.speciesDesc: Heterostemma bicanthaceum is a species of flowering plant in the family Asclepiadaceae. It is endemic to New Caledonia and is characterized by its unique rail-like structures on the gynostegium, which are composed of extended lateral appendages of the staminal corona lobes. The species has been found in various habitats, including coastal gallery forests, moist primary forests, and calcareous rocky soil.

images.captionText: FIGURE 1. A, B, Heterostemma bicanthaceum (A, Ile des Pins, Sentier Oro-Upi, 20-04-16, GG756, photo: G. Gâteblé; B, Ile des Pins, 20-04-2016, photo: C. Laudereau). C, D, Marsdenia kuniensis (C, Ile des Pins, Sentier Oro-Upi, 20 Apr 2016, GG754). E, F, Marsdenia neomicrostoma (Grande Terre, Tinip, 08 Mar 2007). (Photo: A,C, E–F: G. Gâteblé; B, D: C. Laudereau).
